content.text: What Are Python Strings

A Python string (type str) is an immutable sequence of Unicode code points. Every character in a Python string is a Unicode character — there is no separate 'char' type, and strings are not byte arrays. Python 3 enforced this cleanly: str is text, bytes is raw binary data, and the two are strictly separate. You must encode a str to get bytes and decode bytes to get a str.

Strings are sequences, which means they support indexing (s[0]), slicing (s[1:5]), len(), in, and iteration. Because strings are immutable, no string method modifies the original — they always return a new string. This immutability makes strings hashable and thus usable as dictionary keys and set members.

Python has three formatting systems: the legacy %-formatting (inherited from C printf), the str.format() method introduced in Python 2.6, and f-strings (formatted string literals) introduced in Python 3.6. F-strings are the modern choice for almost all use cases: they are faster, more readable, and support expressions, method calls, and format specifiers directly in the literal.
Why Strings and Formatting Matter

Strings are the primary input and output format for language models. Every prompt you send to an LLM is a string; every response you receive is a string. Parsing, cleaning, and constructing strings correctly is central to building reliable agentic systems. A malformed prompt or an incorrectly parsed response can cause downstream failures that are hard to debug.

String formatting is also the most common source of injection vulnerabilities in software. SQL injection, prompt injection, and template injection all arise from mixing untrusted data into string templates without proper sanitisation. Understanding how Python's formatting mechanisms work — and when to use them — is a prerequisite for writing secure applications.
Benefits
Immutability makes strings safe to share between threads and safe to use as dict keys without defensive copying.
F-strings evaluate expressions at the point of use: f'{len(items)} items, avg={sum(items)/len(items):.2f}' is self-contained.
Format specifiers (:.2f, :>10, :,) produce formatted output without manual arithmetic.
Python's rich string method library covers almost all common text-processing needs without third-party dependencies.
Unicode support is built in: emoji, accented characters, CJK symbols all work without special handling.
Raw strings (r'...') make regular expressions readable by disabling backslash escape processing.
Multiline strings (triple quotes) preserve literal newlines, making long prompts and templates readable.
Trade-offs

String concatenation in a loop is O(n^2) because each + creates a new string. When building a string from many pieces, collect them in a list and join at the end: result = ''.join(parts). This is O(n) and much faster for large n.

F-strings evaluate their expressions at runtime, which means they are not suitable as static templates that can be filled in later. Use str.format() or string.Template when you need to define a template once and fill values in later from different call sites.

Unicode normalisation: two strings that look identical on screen may compare unequal if they use different Unicode normalisation forms (NFC vs. NFD). When comparing user-supplied text for equality, normalise first with unicodedata.normalize('NFC', text).
How Strings Work
String literals

Python recognises four string delimiter styles: single quotes ('...'), double quotes ("..."), triple single quotes ('''...'''), and triple double quotes ("""..."""). Single and double quotes are interchangeable; choose whichever requires fewer escape sequences inside the string. Triple-quoted strings span multiple lines and include all literal newlines.

String prefixes modify how the literal is interpreted: r'...' is a raw string (backslashes are not escape sequences), b'...' is a bytes literal, f'...' is a formatted string, and these can be combined: rf'...' is a raw f-string. The prefix is a compile-time directive — there is no runtime cost.
Indexing and slicing

Strings are sequences of Unicode code points. s[0] is the first character, s[-1] is the last. Slicing s[start:stop:step] extracts a substring. All slice indices are clipped silently — s[100:200] on a 10-character string returns '' rather than raising an error. s[::-1] reverses a string.
Encoding and bytes

When you need to write a string to a file, send it over a network, or store it in a database, you must encode it to bytes first: b = text.encode('utf-8'). When you receive bytes from a network or file, decode them to a string: text = b.decode('utf-8'). Always specify the encoding explicitly; never rely on the system default, which varies across platforms.
String System Diagram

attrs.alt: Python string system: indexing diagram, three formatting methods (f-string, str.format, %-format), and essential string methods by category

content.text: When to Use Each Formatting Method
Method
Syntax
Best for
Avoid when
f-string
f'{expr}'
All new code; expressions, format specs, self-documenting
Templates filled in later (f-string runs immediately)
str.format()
'{key}'.format(**d)
Template strings; logging format strings; older Python
Simple one-shot interpolation (f-string is cleaner)
%-formatting
'%s' % val
Legacy codebases only
All new code — error-prone and less readable
string.Template
User-supplied templates (safe; limited substitution)
Complex expressions or format specifiers
How to Implement
String basics, indexing, and methods

# --- String literals and prefixes ---
s1 = "Hello, World!"
s2 = 'it\'s a test'       # single-quote delimiter; escape the apostrophe
s3 = "it's a test"         # double-quote delimiter; no escape needed
s4 = r"C:\Users\Alice"   # raw string: backslashes are literal
s5 = b"binary data"        # bytes literal
s6 = """Multiline
string that spans
three lines."""

# --- Indexing and slicing ---
text = "Hello World"
print(text[0])         # H
print(text[-1])        # d
print(text[6:11])      # World
print(text[:5])        # Hello
print(text[::2])       # HloWrd  (every second character)
print(text[::-1])      # dlroW olleH  (reversed)

# --- Immutability ---
# text[0] = 'h'  # TypeError: 'str' object does not support item assignment
modified = text.replace("Hello", "Hi")  # returns new string
print(text)      # Hello World  (unchanged)
print(modified)  # Hi World

# --- Case methods ---
s = "hello world"
print(s.upper())        # HELLO WORLD
print(s.title())        # Hello World
print(s.capitalize())   # Hello world
print("HELLO".lower())  # hello
print("Hello".swapcase())  # hELLO

# --- Search methods ---
text = "the quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog"
print(text.find("fox"))         # 16  (first index, or -1 if not found)
print(text.index("fox"))        # 16  (raises ValueError if not found)
print(text.count("the"))        # 2
print(text.startswith("the"))   # True
print(text.endswith("dog"))     # True

# --- Split / join / strip ---
csv_line = "  Alice,30,London  "
stripped = csv_line.strip()     # remove leading/trailing whitespace
fields = stripped.split(",")    # ['Alice', '30', 'London']
rejoined = " | ".join(fields)   # "Alice | 30 | London"

lines = "line1\nline2\nline3"
print(lines.splitlines())       # ['line1', 'line2', 'line3']

# Partition: splits on first occurrence, returns 3-tuple
key, sep, value = "host=localhost".partition("=")
print(key, value)   # host localhost

# --- Replace and translate ---
text = "banana"
print(text.replace("a", "o"))   # bonono (all occurrences)
print(text.replace("a", "o", 2))  # bonona (max 2 replacements)

# Remove vowels using translate + maketrans
table = str.maketrans("aeiou", "     ")  # map vowels to spaces
print("Hello World".translate(table).replace(" ", ""))  # Hll Wrld

# --- Test methods (return bool) ---
print("123".isdigit())       # True
print("abc".isalpha())       # True
print("abc123".isalnum())    # True
print("   ".isspace())       # True
print("Title Case".istitle()) # True
F-strings and str.format() with format specifiers

# --- F-string basics ---
name = "Alice"
age = 30
pi = 3.14159265

# Simple interpolation
print(f"Name: {name}, Age: {age}")

# Expressions inside braces
print(f"In 5 years: {age + 5}")
print(f"Uppercase: {name.upper()}")
print(f"Length: {len(name)}")

# --- Format specifiers ---
# Floats
print(f"{pi:.2f}")          # 3.14 (2 decimal places)
print(f"{pi:10.3f}")        # "     3.142" (width 10, 3 dp)
print(f"{pi:e}")            # 3.141593e+00 (scientific)

# Integers
n = 1_000_000
print(f"{n:,}")             # 1,000,000 (thousands separator)
print(f"{n:_}")             # 1_000_000 (underscore separator)
print(f"{255:#x}")          # 0xff (hex with prefix)
print(f"{255:08b}")         # 11111111 (8-digit binary)

# Strings
print(f"{'left':<10}|")    # 'left      |' (left-align, width 10)
print(f"{'right':>10}|")   # '     right|' (right-align)
print(f"{'center':^10}|")  # '  center  |' (centered)
print(f"{'fill':*^10}|")   # '**fill****|' (fill char *)

# Percent
score = 0.875
print(f"{score:.1%}")      # 87.5%

# Debug: {value=} prints name and value
x = 42
print(f"{x=}")             # x=42 (Python 3.8+)

# Nested f-string (format width from variable)
width = 12
for value in [1.1, 22.22, 333.333]:
    print(f"{value:{width}.3f}")

# --- str.format() ---
template = "Hello, {}! You have {} messages."
print(template.format("Bob", 5))

# Named placeholders
tmpl = "Dear {name}, your order #{order_id} is {status}."
print(tmpl.format(name="Alice", order_id=12345, status="shipped"))

# Unpack a dict
context = {"name": "Bob", "order_id": 99, "status": "pending"}
print(tmpl.format(**context))

# Format spec inside str.format
print("{:.2f}".format(3.14159))
print("{:>10}".format("right"))
Multiline strings, encoding, and regex basics

# --- Multiline strings ---
prompt = """
You are a helpful assistant. Answer the following question:

{question}

Please be concise and accurate.
"""

# Note: leading newline from the triple-quote is included.
# Common pattern: use .strip() or textwrap.dedent()
import textwrap

prompt_clean = textwrap.dedent("""
    You are a helpful assistant.
    Answer: {question}
""").strip()

# --- Encoding and bytes ---
text = "Hello, 世界"  # Unicode string with Chinese characters

# Encode to bytes
utf8_bytes = text.encode("utf-8")       # b'Hello, \xe4\xb8\x96\xe7\x95\x8c'
ascii_bytes = "Hello".encode("ascii")   # b'Hello'

print(len(text))       # 9 characters
print(len(utf8_bytes)) # 13 bytes (Chinese chars take 3 bytes each in UTF-8)

# Decode back to string
decoded = utf8_bytes.decode("utf-8")
print(decoded == text)   # True

# --- Regular expressions (basics) ---
import re

text = "Contact us at support@example.com or admin@company.org"

# Search for a pattern
m = re.search(r"[\w.]+@[\w.]+", text)
if m:
    print(m.group())    # support@example.com (first match)

# Find all matches
emails = re.findall(r"[\w.]+@[\w.]+", text)
print(emails)   # ['support@example.com', 'admin@company.org']

# Substitute
cleaned = re.sub(r"[\w.]+@[\w.]+", "[REDACTED]", text)
print(cleaned)  # "Contact us at [REDACTED] or [REDACTED]"

# Groups
pattern = r"(\w+)@(\w+)\.([\w.]+)"
for m in re.finditer(pattern, text):
    print(f"User: {m.group(1)}, Domain: {m.group(2)}.{m.group(3)}")

# Compile for reuse
email_re = re.compile(r"[\w.+-]+@[\w-]+\.[\w.]+")
is_email = bool(email_re.fullmatch("test@example.com"))
print(is_email)  # True
Real-world Example: Prompt Builder for an LLM Agent

Building reliable prompts for language models is a string-heavy task. Prompt templates combine static text with dynamic context: conversation history, tool descriptions, retrieved documents. Proper string handling ensures prompts are correctly formatted, stay within token limits, and do not accidentally inject control sequences.

import textwrap
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class Message:
    role: str
    content: str

def format_message(msg: Message) -> str:
    """Format a single conversation message."""
    role_label = msg.role.upper()
    # Indent continuation lines for readability
    wrapped = textwrap.fill(msg.content, width=80, subsequent_indent="  ")
    return f"{role_label}: {wrapped}"

def build_prompt(
    system: str,
    history: list[Message],
    user_input: str,
    tools: list[str],
    max_history_chars: int = 2000,
) -> str:
    """Build a well-structured LLM prompt."""
    # Format tool list as a bulleted section
    tool_section = "\n".join(f"  - {tool}" for tool in tools)

    # Trim history to stay within budget
    history_text_parts = [format_message(m) for m in history]
    history_text = "\n\n".join(history_text_parts)
    if len(history_text) > max_history_chars:
        # Keep the most recent messages that fit
        trimmed = []
        budget = max_history_chars
        for part in reversed(history_text_parts):
            if budget - len(part) < 0:
                break
            trimmed.insert(0, part)
            budget -= len(part) + 2
        history_text = "\n\n".join(trimmed)

    # F-string builds the final prompt with multiline template
    prompt = f"""
{system.strip()}

Available tools:
{tool_section}

Conversation so far:
{history_text}

USER: {user_input.strip()}

ASSISTANT:"""
    return textwrap.dedent(prompt).strip()

# Example usage
system_msg = "You are a helpful research assistant with access to web search and a calculator."
history = [
    Message("user", "What is the population of Tokyo?"),
    Message("assistant", "Tokyo has approximately 13.96 million people in the city proper."),
]
tools = ["web_search(query: str) -> str", "calculator(expression: str) -> float"]
user_input = "And what percentage of Japan's total population does that represent?"

prompt = build_prompt(system_msg, history, user_input, tools)
print(prompt)
print(f"\nPrompt length: {len(prompt)} characters")
Common Mistakes
Concatenating strings in a loop: result += item is O(n^2). Use ''.join(parts) instead.
Confusing str and bytes: text = b'hello'.decode('utf-8') is required — you cannot index bytes and get a character.
Using str.format() or %-formatting and forgetting to escape % signs: '%s: 100%%' (% needs doubling in %-format).
Not specifying encoding in encode()/decode(): open('f', 'r') uses the system locale encoding, which differs across platforms. Always use encoding='utf-8'.
Using regex when str methods suffice: text.startswith('http') is faster and clearer than re.match(r'^http', text).
Treating len(s) as the number of bytes: len('世界') == 2, but '世界'.encode('utf-8') has 6 bytes.
Forgetting that f-string expressions cannot span multiple lines without backslash continuation or a helper variable (Python 3.11 relaxed this in some cases).
Best Practices
Prefer f-strings for all new formatting code — they are fast, readable, and support full expressions.
Use ''.join(parts) instead of += in loops to avoid O(n^2) concatenation.
Always specify encoding='utf-8' in open() and encode()/decode() calls.
Use textwrap.dedent() on triple-quoted strings when the content should not have leading indentation.
Compile regular expressions used in loops with re.compile() to avoid recompiling on each iteration.
Use raw strings (r'...') for regular expressions to avoid double-escaping backslashes.
Validate and sanitise user-supplied strings before interpolating them into prompts, SQL, or shell commands.
Use str.encode('utf-8', errors='replace') for robustness when encoding text that may contain unmappable characters.
Performance and Scalability

String immutability means every concatenation creates a new object. For five strings being joined, this is negligible. For 100,000 strings in a log aggregation loop, it is the difference between milliseconds and seconds. The ''.join() pattern is the Python idiom for efficient multi-string concatenation and should be your default when building strings from many pieces.

F-string evaluation is slightly faster than str.format() because it uses specialised LOAD_FAST opcodes compiled at parse time, whereas str.format() dispatches through the object's __format__ method at runtime. For tight loops building many strings, this matters; for typical application code, both are fast enough.

For very large text processing (gigabyte log files, large corpora), process data line-by-line using a generator rather than loading the entire file into a string. Use io.StringIO for in-memory string streams, and mmap for memory-mapped file access.
Tooling and Ecosystem
re module (stdlib): regular expressions — search, findall, sub, split, compile, and named groups.
textwrap module (stdlib): wrap, fill, dedent, indent, shorten — for formatting text for terminal output.
string module (stdlib): Template class, ascii_letters, digits, punctuation constants.
unicodedata module (stdlib): normalize(), category(), name() — for Unicode-aware text processing.
chardet / charset-normalizer (third-party): detect encoding of bytes from unknown sources.
Jinja2 (third-party): full-featured template engine — safer than f-strings for user-supplied templates.
ftfy (third-party): fixes broken Unicode text (mojibake, smart quotes, etc.).
Comparison of Formatting Methods
Feature
f-string
str.format()
%-formatting
string.Template
Speed
Fastest
Fast
Slowest
Fast
Expressions
Yes (full)
Limited (method calls)
No
No
Deferred template
No
Yes
Yes
Yes
Format specifiers
Full
Full
Limited

Injection safety
Python expression only
As safe as input
As safe as input

Python version
All
Readability
Best
Good
Poor
Good
Security and Reliability
Prompt injection: an attacker can embed instructions in user-supplied text that override your system prompt. Sanitise or clearly separate user-supplied text from trusted prompt instructions.
Never use format() or f-strings to build SQL queries — use parameterised queries (cursor.execute(sql, params)) instead.
Avoid str.format(**user_dict) with untrusted dictionaries — users can access object attributes via format spec tricks like {0.__class__.__name__}.
Use string.Template for user-supplied templates — it supports only ${key} substitution and cannot access object attributes or evaluate expressions.
Re-encode and validate strings at trust boundaries: when accepting text from an external system, normalise to NFC Unicode and strip control characters before processing.
Mental Model

Think of a Python string as a sequence of Unicode code points, not as bytes. The encoding (UTF-8, ASCII, Latin-1) is only relevant when you transfer the string across a boundary — to a file, network, or database. Inside Python, all strings are the same: Unicode text. The str/bytes distinction enforces this boundary explicitly, making encoding bugs visible at the point of conversion rather than silently corrupting data.

For formatting, think of f-strings as embedded expressions that are evaluated at the exact line where they appear. The brace syntax {expression:format_spec} is a mini-language: the part before the colon is any Python expression, the part after the colon is a format specifier that controls width, precision, alignment, and representation. Mastering format specifiers eliminates most hand-written number-formatting code.
Key Takeaways
Python strings are immutable sequences of Unicode code points — not bytes.
Use f-strings for all new formatting code: f'{name!r} has {len(name)} chars'.
Format specifiers control width, precision, alignment, and base: f'{value:.2f}', f'{n:,}', f'{s:>10}'.
Build strings from many pieces with ''.join(parts), not += in a loop (O(n^2) vs O(n)).
Encode to bytes before writing to file or network: text.encode('utf-8'); decode when reading: b.decode('utf-8').
Use raw strings (r'...') for regular expressions to avoid double-escaping backslashes.
The re module provides search, findall, sub, split, and fullmatch; compile patterns reused in loops.
Use string.Template or Jinja2 for user-supplied templates — f-strings and str.format(**user_dict) can be exploited.
